item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, Nixon has the scandal (or some other event that didn't end up happening because the ritual failed) , and his failure lays the groundwork for the next 3. Ford pardons Nixon, wiping up the sins and burying the bones. I'm not sure about the rest, though.

EDIT: Just realized that Reagan must have gone against their agenda, thus the creation of the Cut Up While Talking tape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really enjoyed this! Nixon's character development was just a pleasure to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How have I not heard of this skip until now? The storytelling here is amazing, and how it simultaneously covers conspiracy theories, history, and the weird occult stuff going on. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Theme of Nixon looks like punch line. But this article is not only Nixon.
I feel 2736 is ordinary man. I love such a sad-inside story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's been a while since you said you would take out the addendum regarding Nixon's political history post-1951, but you haven't. What's interesting is that you put the Apollo 11 event there even though it has nothing do with the skip.

Also, there isn't anything in the profile that says how the Foundation managed to find out who the rest of the four Kings are.

The first falls on his sword, but his blood sows the seeds for the next thousand years. The second wipes up all the sins, and no one finds the gnawed bones under the castle. The third smiles at the world with double-edged teeth, and his laugh opens the floodgates. The fourth defies the masters and forever pays the price.

I wonder what this means historically. I'm pretty rusty with my US history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

I've always been fascinated by U.S. presidents as well, especially Nixon. I'd been wanting to write an article about him for a while, and the History Contest struck me as the perfect opportunity. Very glad you enjoyed it.

Hey, my first +100! Sweet!

A huge thanks to all of you who read and voted. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Seems that I am the one who made your article go from +99 to +100. Very good for a first SCP. I am fascinated by American presidents, and it is nice to see an SCP related to one of the most strangest ones in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your thoughts! Glad you liked it, and I'll keep your suggestions in mind when I get the time to rework the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say that this is an excellent piece. My only suggestions are to lose or justify the timeline and make the fact it's something of a companion piece to SCP-1981 a bit more explicit.

+1.

(Also, WrongJohnSilver's analysis hits the nail on the head for me. Making it more "new age 2010 horror cult" would betray the piece by divorcing it from the period tropes.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I'll keep that in mind. And as for the "Level 2 Clearance" thing, I had my reasons for including it, but yeah, I could probably do without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks very much, Buttfranklin!

And come on, folks – two Richard Nixons, naked as big, hammy babes, crawling out of the bowels of the Earth and bare-knuckle brawling to the death. What an image. If every religious ritual ended like that, maybe I wouldn't have stopped going to Mass.

I love that comment so much. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, WrongJohn!

That, combined with some crisp dated dialogue ("You've got some nerve!") makes the entire piece feel real.

Really glad you picked up on the dated dialogue, I really made an effort to have it feel era-appropriate. I also tried to imagine every line of dialogue I wrote being spoken by Nixon, to see if it was the kind of thing he'd say. The "You've got some nerve!" line is probably my favorite, but I also enjoyed making him say "Real big-wigs".

Replace the hokey ritual with something edgier, more bizarre, more in keeping with the 2010s zeitgeist, and it wouldn't work nearly as well.

Yes, the fact that the ritual takes place in the 50s is the reason I took a cheesier approach to that scene - it's the kind of thing that would have gone on at that time. Even if that stuff isn't scary to the modern reader, it was scary back then.

how the hell did someone ever think this could even have been a possibility? But believe they did.

Yep, and stuff like that is still happening.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Thanks very much!

In my opinion, that's intentional.

You guessed right, the cliché-ness of the cult was deliberate on my part - although I hadn't foreseen it would be such a problem for some readers (but I get it). But yeah, as I say up-thread, by using such an unoriginal GoI I was aiming for realism, which I felt was necessary to help sell the (rather preposterous) premise of the skip to readers and help suspend disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Eta! Welcome back to the Internet!

No worries about not being able to help with this, I totally get it, you have nothing to apologize for. And thanks a lot for your in-depth review, you make some excellent points (especially regarding the GoI's name). This will come in very useful when I rework the article after the contest - hopefully I'll be able to address all of your concerns… although I probably won't be turning this into a novel!1 ;)

Footnotes
1. But seriously, I get your point. This is something that could have easily gone on for much longer if I hadn't forced myself to condense the story into as few scenes as I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks psul, glad you enjoyed it. And your concerns re: the clichéd cult behavior are noted - I'll see what I can do post-contest.

The build-up to the 1981 connection was well-executed, it feels like it expands the crosslink rather than leaning on it

I'm very happy you think so, as this is what I was aiming for specifically. SCP-1981 is an article I'm particularly fond of, and I wanted to try to add to it, as well as borrow from it. I also tried to build the skip around the cross-link, and not the other way around, and to write allusions to 1981 into every aspect of the skip, in the hopes of adding depth to the connection between both articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For those that are worried about the cheesiness of this all, look up the Satanic scares of the late 70s/early 80s. For a while, people were legitimately terrified of cults consisting of their neighbors performing blasphemous rituals in their own hometowns, or which politicians and celebrities were secretly in league with the devil. Looking back, you can only think how the hell did someone ever think this could even have been a possibility? But believe they did.

That, combined with some crisp dated dialogue ("You've got some nerve!") makes the entire piece feel real. Replace the hokey ritual with something edgier, more bizarre, more in keeping with the 2010s zeitgeist, and it wouldn't work nearly as well.

Well done. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thought it was pretty cheesy, but earned a +1 for the tie-ins to 1981. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As an international reader, having the timeline included as a collapsible was nice. Though it's probably not necessary to limit publically available information to level 2 employees. You can just make it a collapsible labelled 'abridged timeline of SCP-2736-2's political career' or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thirded, for what it's worth. Sometimes clichés are clichés because they do actually happen in real life.

Plus, I guess there's still a part of me that's a sucker for boilerplate spoopy ritual shit. I liked that Bohemian Grove transcript on its own merits. Well-written, packed with genuinely creepy details, good dialogue, A+ disfigurement.

And come on, folks – two Richard Nixons, naked as big, hammy babes, crawling out of the bowels of the Earth and bare-knuckle brawling to the death. What an image. If every religious ritual ended like that, maybe I wouldn't have stopped going to Mass.

I get the criticisms of this article. I really do. It is a stupid trope. It is really cliche.

In my opinion, that's intentional.

The whole article plays out like a decent, well-written version of an old horror movie. It's fun. It's entertaining. It's everything I look for in this kind of SCP. The 1981 connection works and I can even forgive the double-trouble Object Class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Herc!

SO sorry I could't help with this… It was right in the middle of my house move and Internetless period!

Okay. Several things:

+ show block
– hide block

- I don't much like 'Magog's Multitude' either. Not a faith-breaker for me, but it seems out of place. I'd imagine this sort of group to take a more modernly powerful name (if you see what I mean…!). And would they name themselves 'Multitude', instead of enhancing their 'select few' status?
Why not keep 'The Bohemian Club'? After all, you do mention Bohemian Grove…! (though I do think 'Bohemian Club' is rather out of character too…)
(How about something like the Knights of the Future… or the Knights of the Space Age… I know, it's not good, but you see what I mean!)

- excellent characterization as usual

- I do agree that you should ditch the timeline. People either know about it or can search for it.

- Perhaps try to make it shorter? It is rather long, and some details could be shortened, I think.

- It's one of those SCPs that is a good SCP, clinical tone included ;>, BUT… it would be better as a short story. Or a long story. Even a novel.
You see, this story makes me want to see and know more about what's been going on all those years: -1's reactions to every event in -2's political career. His rant about the Watergate. His perpetual sickness at imagining the guy who tried to kill him for power in bed with his wife. His thoughts about the next presidents, their personalities, their policies…
All this is of course implied or can be imagined, but the clinical tone prevents it from appearing at any time, and SCPs cannot be novel-length…!

- I wonder if you could show somewhere, just in passing, that though -2 looks exactly like -1, he seems… darker, or something? And that after (and despite) containment -1 was generally a pleasant man? You do establish pretty quickly that -2 is the powermad side of RN, but you don't go along with it - pity, I think.

Okay. Anyway. +1 with special congratulations from the jury!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree in part with some of the criticism of the cult-behaviour - I think moving away from the clichéd imagery and adding something weirder (or just leaving out those details) would help smooth the early going here.

However I think this is a skip that gets better and better as it goes on. The build-up to the 1981 connection was well-executed, it feels like it expands the crosslink rather than leaning on it, and the photo makes a great punchline. And you managed to make me feel slightly sorry for Richard Nixon, so there's that.

Very enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my, so good. Not to complex but still hits that cult creep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered including a Solidus Nixon in my summary, but decided that since -1 had all the Good Nixon Traits and -2 had all the Bad Nixon Traits, a Solid/Liquid comparison was more apt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay but, which one of them is the werewolf?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't -2 be Solidus Nixon, since he became president?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! :)

You might be interested to know that Nixon also made the following statement (which I can't help but find ominous in the context of this skip) about Bohemian Grove:

“If I were to choose the speech that gave me the most pleasure and satisfaction in my political career, it would be my Lakeside Speech at the Bohemian Grove in July 1967. Because this speech traditionally was off the record it received no publicity at the time. But in many important ways it marked the first milestone on my road to the presidency.”—President Richard Nixon, Memoirs (1978).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks very much for the kind words, Zacharia. I'm very glad you enjoyed this. :)

2736-1's last words made me grimace with satisfaction. I even felt a shiver coming on.

;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for such an in-depth review, Shaggy. I think you're pretty much right on every point, and I'll keep all of that in mind when I make changes to this after the contest. :)

item.title: Re: Magog's Multitude
item.content:encoded:
Whoa, I had no idea this was a real thing.

As I said up-thread, I chose the word for its affiliation with the Skull and Bones organization, and also because the biblical cities of Gog and Magog are known as the enemy of God, and have apocalyptic connotations, all of which I thought was rather appropriate for the GOI. As for the "multitude" part, I just made it up - I suppose it's always possible I subconsciously remembered hearing the term from somewhere, but I doubt it. Neat coincidence.

Thanks for sharing this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact, Richard Nixon was once quoted saying that Bohemian Grove was "The most faggy god damned thing you could imagine". I think this is one of the best examples of weird real world history in the scp universe, but I do agree that some of it is just a pinch too melodramatic, but ultimately its a +1 from me, very well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the same way. This was enjoyable to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Presidential clones, neo-pagan cults and government conspiracies - all in one place, without breaking suspension of disbelief. Wow-wee.

The interviews are where I find the most value here; this is top-notch writing and characterisation, and the historical details feel authentic in context. The link with SCP-1981 is the pretty little ribbon that ties it all together quite nicely, with some disturbing implications.1

The way I see it, your two Nixons have more zing than sixty Laikas. If it was up to me to pick the contest winner out of all the entries so far, I'd pick this one.

+1

Footnotes
1. 2736-1's last words made me grimace with satisfaction. I even felt a shiver coming on.
Everything after the first collapsible just kind of exists because it has to, but it's not that great. Everything before then is golden.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I should have specified. I was referring to the last sentence of SCP-1981's description (just before the first collapsible), which I believe served as the inspiration for Cut Up While Thinking, a tale I've very much headcanoned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No voting for now, I feel like this goes on a little longer than it needs to, and could use trimmed down in places. The surveillance log, for starters, could stand to be shortened some. I'd comb over it once or twice and see if there's any unnecessary bits of wordage that can go.

I would say that Addendum 2736-01 isn't all that important to the piece, and can be axed.

I think the third interview could go, personally. It is a lot of words taking up space to tell us what can already be inferred (broken ritual led to -2's/the GOI's failures) from the rest of the article.

Aside from the length, I enjoyed this. The whole story was rather satisfying. I actually had no issues with the GOI being spoopy, and in fact, appreciated the role they fill.

It's a very down-to-Earth view of the Foundation, where they aren't ludicrously powerful and employing para/magitech to solve every issue. The GOI is in a rather comfortable niche that isn't really possible with other, more fantastical GOI's in that they use (for the most part) non-anomalous means to pose a threat. The fact that they're sitting on the right side of the veil gives them a healthy advantage. I could see a couple good stories coming out of them.

The last bit, damn. It almost cinched my upvote. The quote at <12:11> especially, was really, very interesting. It shifted the whole perspective for what's going on with the GOI/what it implies regarding -2.

Upon a reread, the length seems for the most part justified considering everything that's going on in this, it could maybe do with some minor revision, but it's overall worthy of an upvote. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All I can say is I don't really remember the "tormenting" part of 1981.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it feels too same-y when compared to some of the other American politics SCP's (1981 and 2078), especially on the deathbed.

Yes, but I'll just point out that this was very deliberate on my part. I was trying to imply that all these skips are interconnected, that they all deal with the same creepy occult forces that seem to drive American politics in the SCP universe. I'll work on maybe making that more explicit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's Richard Nixon, Cut Up, Then Talking

Ha, I like it!

the inclusion of the timeline of Nixon's political career, which doesn't add anything

You know what, I agree. I only included it to help readers who weren't familiar with Nixon, but really all you learn from this is that he was president and that the Watergate scandal was a thing, which I think everyone knows. I'll probably just take it out post-contest.

the final picture, which seems to exist only so you can link to 1981.

The 1981 reference is definitely not meant to feel shoehorned - there are actually several references to 1981 throughout the text before that point, but they might simply not be clear/obvious enough. I'll see what I can do about that. My intent in linking 1981 was to suggest that the same forces that tormented Reagan at the end of his life are the ones tormenting Nixon at the end of his (and those forces are what put both those men in power).

Otherwise, this is a really great skip, especially for a first!

Thanks, I really appreciate that! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: MrWrong is right (that was fun to type), they're meant to be the four kings. I'll try to think of something to make that clearer, though. And as for the timeline collapsible, you're right, I'll probably just take it out post-contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a bad idea - a lot of people seem to dislike the current name. I might change it to that after the contest. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Cyan, glad you enjoyed it! :)

it's a stark reminder of how, in the Foundation universe, no one is safe from the world of anomalous effects and events

I honestly hadn't thought about it like that before, but I like it.

there is one thing I don't like, and that's the random new GoI.

Is it the fact that it's a new GOI that's bothering you (and you think I should have used one of the preexisting ones), or is it that you're not crazy about what they are?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. All right, I'll mull this over and see what I can change after the contest, while staying true to the spirit of the piece, as Deci put it. I doubt I'll be able to please everybody, since some readers clearly like the (realistic) cheesiness, but I wonder whether there might be a way to balance out the GOI's penchant for theatricality with a more original approach to the ritual.

item.title: Magog's Multitude
item.content:encoded: For those complaining that Magog's Multitude is cheesy, it actually appears in one of the biblical apocrypha: https://books.google.com/books?id=8JkHAAAAQAAJ&lpg=PA55&ots=buLo0uZs6S&dq=magog's%20multitude&pg=PA55#v=onepage&q=magog's%20multitude&f=false

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If my guess is correct, they should be the Four Kings from Nixon's delirium ramble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this article, but I have to agree with TL333's criticisms. The timeline could easily be cut and not impact the article, and the last collapsible was confusing. Were you trying to imply something by making Ford, Carter, and Reagan in to PoI, other than presidents being of interest to the Foundation in general?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gee wiz, how come your skip lets you have two Richard Nixons?

In all seriousness, I think this was an interesting concept, refining the power seeking part of someone by splitting out from the base personality and then making one kill the other. Additionally, having the ritual fall apart because it couldn't be completed, and resulting in Richard Nixon's eventual disgrace is a neat way of tying in the anomalous world to an interesting historical figure.

I'm not bothered by the 2spooky aspect of the GoI, though I will agree that it does come across as corny. However, that really didn't do much to knock this off the rails for me, as it seems to for other readers.

Either way +1. Neat skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ridiculous premise. Great execution. I love the fact that this made me feel for a politician who I had never really felt much for other than 'hey, that guy did some stuff.'

Take your +1. You deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The cornball-hellfire-club nonsense being as melodramatic and cheesy as it is in real life is what cements my vote, just for the record.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this as a draft and I love it here. As I said earlier, the group is somewhat wacky and it's not the most original (though I didn't know it was a real organization), but that's entirely unrelated to the great writing the rest of the piece offers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I said all that I could say already. It's one of those things that give off impact and offer a ride for readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked this more than expected. The idea of the faux-spooky Bohemian Grove hiding an actual evil cult is almost entertaining enough to make up for "Magog's Multitude".
The image of Nixon split into two, unequal men is also well done. That -1 was convinced that -2 wasn't *really* Nixon - but was the one *being* Nixon - gives this a neatly executed mind screw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. When the contest is over, I'd try and revise it so that you keep the spirit of this while not making it seem so cornball like it is in real life. This is what's keeping me from upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's Richard Nixon, Cut Up, Then Talking

Actually, my two criticisms would be the inclusion of the timeline of Nixon's political career, which doesn't add anything, and the final picture, which seems to exist only so you can link to 1981. Otherwise, this is a really great skip, especially for a first!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2736-1 is Solid Nixon, and SCP-2736-2 is Liquid Nixon. They are both clones of the original, Big Senator, also known as Naked Nixon, who used his stealth skills to escape that pit undetected and now wears a cool eyepatch since they cut his eye out. It all lines up.

That aside, I think I'll novote this for now. The concept is good, and some parts of it are great, but the plot of the piece, to me, falls a bit short. "The ritual wasn't actually completed" isn't all that compelling of a plot — it's more of a plot hook than a beat itself. The babbling and creepy imagery don't really work for me, so I'm left with a skip that has a stable foundation, but doesn't go anywhere truly exemplary with it. It doesn't go all-out with the cult, nor with the ritual, nor with the creepiness, nor even with the characterization of either Nixon. It sort of goes halfway in every direction, so at the end I'm left with a feeling of vague unsatisfaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept of the twin Nixons - I didn't know what to expect when I saw the picture, but I got something great.
I'm not sure where to stand on the spooky talk, though
It's incredibly cheesy, which I like, but it feels too same-y when compared to some of the other American politics SCP's (1981 and 2078), especially on the deathbed.

Despite that, I'm giving this a lukewarm +1 because the occulto-babble doesn't ruin my overall enjoyment of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't know that the Bohemian Grove (and its attendees) was really like that; that doesn't really change my opinion, but it's interesting nonetheless.

I wanted realism in order to make Nixon's situation seem more believable.

Yeah, I suppose I can see where you're coming from on this front. Thing is, while truth can be stranger than fiction, truth can also be shittier than fiction. This is a situation where I would not hesitate to use as much artistic license as possible to make up for the global elite's penchant towards being really fucking lame.
